title: Quiz: Quantum Supremacy
description: Test your understanding of quantum supremacy, sampling problems, and verification challenges

questions.question: What distinguishes quantum supremacy from quantum advantage?

options.text: Supremacy requires error correction, advantage does not
Supremacy is for any task, advantage is for useful tasks
Supremacy requires polynomial speedup, advantage requires exponential
They are the same concept with different names

questions.explanation: Quantum supremacy refers to demonstrating quantum advantage on ANY computational task (even artificial ones). Quantum advantage typically refers to speedup on practical, useful problems. Supremacy is easier to achieve because the task need not be useful.

questions.question: What is the linear cross-entropy benchmark (XEB) measuring?

options.text: The entropy of the quantum state
How well samples match the ideal distribution
The number of gates in the circuit
The error rate of individual gates

questions.explanation: XEB measures sampling quality by computing 2^n * E[P(x)] - 1, where P(x) is the ideal probability of each sample. XEB = 1 for perfect sampling and XEB = 0 for uniform random guessing.

questions.question: What is the Porter-Thomas distribution in the context of random circuits?

options.text: The distribution of gate errors
The distribution of output probabilities for random circuits
The distribution of circuit depths
The distribution of qubit coherence times

questions.explanation: For sufficiently deep random circuits, the output probabilities follow a Porter-Thomas (exponential) distribution P(p) = N*exp(-Np), where N = 2^n. This 'anti-concentration' property means probabilities are spread across many outcomes.

questions.question: If random circuit sampling could be done efficiently classically, the polynomial hierarchy would collapse to its third level.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: True. The complexity-theoretic argument for RCS hardness shows that efficient classical approximate sampling would imply PH collapse. Since this is widely believed to be false, RCS is believed to be classically hard.

questions.question: What was Google's claim about Sycamore's performance in the 2019 supremacy experiment?

options.text: Sycamore factored a 2048-bit number
Sycamore performed RCS in 200s vs 10,000 years classically
Sycamore achieved 99.99% gate fidelity
Sycamore simulated 100-qubit circuits

questions.explanation: Google claimed Sycamore completed random circuit sampling in 200 seconds, while the best classical supercomputer (Summit) would take approximately 10,000 years. This estimate has since been revised downward by improved classical algorithms.

questions.question: What is the main challenge in verifying quantum supremacy claims?

options.text: Quantum computers are too slow to produce enough samples
We cannot classically compute the ideal distribution to compare against
All quantum computers have identical outputs
Classical computers can easily verify by brute force

questions.explanation: The verification problem: if we cannot classically compute the ideal output distribution (that's the whole point of supremacy), how do we verify the quantum computer is sampling correctly? This requires indirect methods like XEB extrapolation.

questions.question: Which is NOT a proposed quantum supremacy experiment?

options.text: Random circuit sampling
Boson sampling
Shor's algorithm for factoring
Gaussian boson sampling

questions.explanation: Shor's algorithm is for factoring and would demonstrate quantum advantage on a useful problem. Supremacy experiments (RCS, boson sampling, GBS) focus on sampling problems that are hard classically but not necessarily useful.

questions.question: Which statements about current quantum supremacy status are accurate? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Classical algorithms have improved since Google's 2019 claim
Supremacy has been achieved for useful optimization problems
Multiple groups have demonstrated sampling-based supremacy
The XEB fidelity achieved by Sycamore was about 0.2%

questions.explanation: Classical algorithms have improved (reducing estimated classical time), multiple groups have demonstrated supremacy (Google, USTC with Jiuzhang), and Sycamore's XEB was about 0.2%. However, no supremacy for useful problems has been achieved yet.
